title: 2 Corinthians 7
subtitle: Paul's Joy at the Church's Repentance

heb.paragraph: The noun hagiōsynē (holiness) in v. 1 denotes the process of being set apart for God. Paul’s exhortation to ‘perfect holiness’ (epitelountes hagiōsynēn) uses the present participle, indicating an ongoing process rather than a one-time achievement. The motivation is ‘reverence for God’ (phobos theou)—the awe before God’s holy presence that drives ethical transformation.

refs.note: Paul’s call to sanctification in Thessalonians provides a parallel to the holiness imperative of 2 Cor 7:1: ‘It is God’s will that you should be sanctified.’
The open-hearts appeal is resumed here after the temple-purity digression, creating a literary bracket.

notes.note: The holiness imperative concludes the temple-purity passage: because the community is God’s temple, it must purify itself from ‘everything that contaminates body and spirit.’ The comprehensive phrase includes both physical and spiritual defilement.

Paul’s triple denial (‘wronged no one, corrupted no one, exploited no one’) anticipates the more detailed defence of chs. 10–13. His expression of confidence and pride in the Corinthians (v. 4) is genuine and prepares for the good news about Titus.

Calvin reads the holiness command as the practical consequence of the temple declaration: those who are God’s dwelling must live accordingly. Holiness is not optional but constitutive of the believer’s identity. Calvin notes Paul’s remarkable combination of confidence (v. 4) with earlier rebuke—a model of pastoral balance.

The NET note on ‘perfecting holiness’ (epitelountes hagiōsynēn) explains the present participle as indicating progressive sanctification: holiness is a process of increasing conformity to God’s character.

Fee reads 7:1 as the conclusion of the 6:14–18 exhortation, while 7:2–4 resumes the emotional appeal of 6:11–13. The combination of holiness imperative and pastoral affection captures the two poles of Paul’s ministry: demand and tenderness.

Harris provides structural analysis showing that 7:1–4 functions as a literary hinge: v. 1 concludes the temple-purity passage, vv. 2–3 resume the emotional appeal, and v. 4 introduces the Titus narrative (vv. 5–16). Harris notes that Paul’s pride in the Corinthians (v. 4) is not merely diplomatic but genuine: their response to the tearful letter has vindicated both Paul’s confidence and his corrective strategy.

hist.context: Chapter 7:1 concludes the temple-purity exhortation of 6:14–18, while 7:2–4 resumes the emotional appeal interrupted at 6:13. Paul reasserts his integrity (‘We have wronged no one, we have corrupted no one, we have exploited no one,’ v. 2)—a triple denial that counters unspecified accusations. His confidence in the Corinthians (vv. 3–4) and his ‘great pride’ in them (v. 4) set the stage for the joyful news about Titus’s report.

heb.paragraph: The noun metanoia (repentance) in vv. 9–10 denotes a fundamental reorientation of mind and will—not mere regret but a decisive turning. Paul distinguishes two kinds of sorrow: ‘godly sorrow’ (lypē kata theon) produces repentance leading to salvation (v. 10a); ‘worldly sorrow’ (lypē tou kosmou) produces death (v. 10b). The distinction is not between feeling sorry and not feeling sorry but between sorrow that leads to transformation and sorrow that leads to despair.

refs.note: Peter’s bitter weeping after denying Jesus exemplifies godly sorrow that leads to repentance and restoration. The contrast with Judas’s despair (Matt 27:3–5) illustrates worldly sorrow that leads to death.
Paul’s earlier instruction to forgive the offender is now contextualised by the Corinthians’ repentance: the tearful letter achieved its purpose, and restoration can proceed.

notes.note: Paul’s arrival in Macedonia was marked by ‘no rest’—conflicts outside, fears within (v. 5). But ‘God, who comforts the downcast’ (v. 6; echoing 1:3–7) comforted him through Titus’s arrival with good news: the Corinthians’ longing for Paul, their deep sorrow, their ardent concern (v. 7). The report transformed Paul’s grief into joy.

Paul’s honesty is disarming: he acknowledges regretting the tearful letter, at least initially (v. 8). But the regret gave way to joy when the letter’s purpose was achieved: repentance. The distinction between godly sorrow and worldly sorrow (v. 10) is one of the most pastorally significant statements in the NT. Godly sorrow is not paralysing guilt but transformative grief that produces change and ‘leaves no regret.’

Calvin treasures the godly-sorrow/worldly-sorrow distinction as a key pastoral diagnostic. Many people feel remorse without genuine repentance: they regret consequences rather than the sin itself. Godly sorrow is different: it produces a change of direction, not merely a change of feeling. Calvin notes that Paul’s temporary regret about the tearful letter (v. 8) demonstrates that even apostles experience uncertainty about the pastoral wisdom of their decisions—a reassurance for ministers who agonise over difficult confrontations.

The NET note on metanoia explains that the term involves more than emotional remorse: it denotes a cognitive and volitional reorientation—a ‘change of mind’ that issues in changed behaviour. The qualifier ‘leading to salvation’ (eis sōtērian) indicates that genuine repentance is a component of the salvific process, not merely a moral improvement.

Fee reads the godly-sorrow passage as evidence that Paul’s confrontational strategy worked: the tearful letter, painful as it was, produced the desired repentance. Fee notes that the distinction between godly and worldly sorrow has implications for the super-apostles’ ministry: their approach may produce temporary feelings without lasting transformation.

Harris provides the most detailed analysis of the godly-sorrow/worldly-sorrow distinction, noting that the Greek construction (kata theon / tou kosmou) denotes the sorrow’s orientation, not its intensity: godly sorrow is sorrow ‘according to God’s will,’ while worldly sorrow is sorrow ‘belonging to the world’s system.’ Harris reads v. 10 as a comprehensive theological statement: repentance and salvation are inseparable, and the sorrow that produces them is a divine gift, not a human achievement.

hist.context: Paul finally resumes the narrative broken off at 2:13: his arrival in Macedonia, his anxiety, and his relief at Titus’s coming with good news from Corinth. The theological centrepiece is the distinction between godly and worldly sorrow (vv. 9–10). Godly sorrow is productive: it leads to repentance, which leads to salvation and ‘leaves no regret.’ Worldly sorrow is destructive: it leads to death. The Corinthians’ response to the tearful letter exemplifies godly sorrow—they were ‘made sorry as God intended’ (v. 9) and demonstrated genuine repentance.

heb.paragraph: The noun spoudē (earnestness, eagerness) in v. 11 heads a catalogue of seven fruits of godly sorrow: earnestness, eagerness to clear themselves, indignation, alarm, longing, concern, and readiness to see justice done. The sevenfold list demonstrates the comprehensiveness of the Corinthians’ repentance: it was not superficial but thorough, affecting their attitudes, emotions, and actions.

refs.note: Titus’s positive experience in Corinth (7:13–15) prepares for his return mission to complete the collection (8:6, 16–17). The relational restoration narrated in ch. 7 makes the financial request of chs. 8–9 possible.
Paul’s language of being ‘refreshed’ (anapauo) by a fellow believer parallels Titus’s refreshment by the Corinthians (7:13).

notes.note: The sevenfold catalogue of repentance-fruits (v. 11) demonstrates the thoroughness of the Corinthians’ response. Paul reveals a deeper purpose for the tearful letter: ‘not on account of the one who did the wrong nor on account of the injured party’ but to demonstrate the Corinthians’ ‘devotion to us’ before God (v. 12). The pastoral goal was relational restoration, not punishment.

Titus’s joy becomes Paul’s joy (v. 13): the apostle’s emotional state is bound to his churches’ wellbeing. Paul’s boasting about the Corinthians has been validated (v. 14)—a fact that matters because the super-apostles have challenged his credibility. The final declaration—‘I can have complete confidence in you’ (v. 16)—is both a statement of fact and a pastoral challenge: live up to this confidence.

Calvin reads the sevenfold catalogue as a diagnostic tool for genuine repentance: true repentance produces not merely sorrow but earnest action, self-examination, and a zeal for righteousness. The purpose statement of v. 12 reveals Paul’s pastoral wisdom: the tearful letter was not about the offender or the offended party but about the community’s relationship to God and to Paul. Calvin notes that Paul’s ‘complete confidence’ (v. 16) is not naive optimism but trust grounded in the demonstrated evidence of repentance.

The NET note identifies the seven fruits of godly sorrow: spoudē (earnestness), apologia (eagerness to clear themselves), aganaktēsis (indignation), phobos (alarm), epipothēsis (longing), zēlos (concern), ekdikēsis (readiness for justice). The catalogue demonstrates comprehensive moral transformation.

Fee reads the conclusion as the emotional resolution of the letter’s first half: the relational crisis between Paul and Corinth has been resolved through the tearful letter, Titus’s mission, and the Corinthians’ repentance. This resolution makes possible the transition to the collection appeal (chs. 8–9) and the renewed polemic (chs. 10–13).

Harris notes that Paul’s declaration of ‘complete confidence’ (v. 16) creates a strategic rhetorical transition: the restored relationship provides the relational capital necessary for the financial request of chs. 8–9 and the more confrontational tone of chs. 10–13. Harris reads the entire chapter as the emotional counterpart to the theological argument of chs. 3–6: the theology of reconciliation (ch. 5) is now embodied in the actual reconciliation of apostle and church.

hist.context: Paul concludes the Titus narrative with unrestrained joy. The Corinthians’ response to the tearful letter has vindicated Paul’s confidence in them and has refreshed Titus’s spirit (v. 13). Paul’s boasting about the Corinthians to Titus (v. 14) has proven justified—a fact that matters deeply to Paul, whose integrity is under attack. The chapter closes with a declaration of ‘complete confidence’ (v. 16) in the Corinthians, which sets the stage for the collection appeal of chs. 8–9.

rows.label: v.1
rows.text: Conclusion of temple-purity exhortation: perfecting holiness

rows.label: vv.2–4
rows.text: Emotional appeal resumed: integrity, confidence, pride

rows.label: vv.5–7
rows.text: Titus’s arrival in Macedonia: comfort after anxiety

rows.label: vv.8–10
rows.text: Godly sorrow vs. worldly sorrow: repentance that leads to salvation

rows.label: vv.11–12
rows.text: Seven fruits of repentance and the letter’s deeper purpose

rows.label: vv.13–16
rows.text: Joy over Titus, vindicated boasting, complete confidence

lit.note: Chapter 7 resolves the emotional narrative suspended since 2:13: Titus brings good news, the Corinthians have repented, and Paul’s confidence is restored. The godly-sorrow distinction (v. 10) is one of the most influential pastoral texts in the NT.

themes.note: Mercy and Holiness dominate as Paul celebrates the Corinthians’ repentance and calls for continued sanctification. The pastoral warmth of the chapter reflects the restored relationship.

hebtext.note: The noun hagiōsynē (holiness) in v. 1 denotes the process of being set apart for God. Paul’s exhortation to ‘perfect holiness’ (epitelountes hagiōsynēn) uses the present participle, indicating an ongoing process rather than a one-time achievement. ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Titus
ppl.text: 4) set the stage for the joyful news about Titus’s report…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Paul's Joy at the Church's Repentance

translations.text: Therefore, since we have these promises, dear friends, let us purify ourselves from everything that contaminates body and spirit, perfecting holiness out of reverence for God.
Since we have these promises, beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from every defilement of body and spirit, bringing holiness to completion in the fear of God.
yet now I am happy, not because you were made sorry, but because your sorrow led you to repentance. For you became sorrowful as God intended and so were not harmed in any way by us.
As it is, I rejoice, not because you were grieved, but because you were grieved into repenting. For you felt a godly grief, so that you suffered no loss through us.

discourse.thesis: Titus brought good news — the Corinthians' godly grief produced repentance, and Paul rejoices in their response.

nodes.text: Since we have these promises, let us cleanse ourselves from every defilement of body and spirit.
Make room for us in your hearts. We have wronged no one. You are in our hearts — I have great confidence and pride in you.
When we came to Macedonia, we had no rest. But God comforted us by the coming of Titus and by your longing for Paul.
Paul's letter grieved them, but he rejoices — not at the grief, but because it led to repentance. Godly grief produces repentance leading to salvation; worldly grief produces death.
We were comforted by Titus's joy. Paul's boasting about them proved true. Titus's affection grows as he remembers their obedience.

coaching.tip: 'Godly grief produces a repentance that leads to salvation without regret, whereas worldly grief produces death' (v. 10). Two kinds of sorrow: one transforms, one destroys. The Corinthians' response to the severe letter was godly grief — earnestness, eagerness, indignation, fear, longing, zeal (v. 11). Repentance proves itself by fruit.

'I rejoice, not because you were grieved, but because you were grieved into repenting' (v. 9). The goal of confrontation is restoration, not humiliation. Paul's relief at their response reveals his pastoral heart. 'In everything you have proved yourselves innocent in this matter' (v. 11). Repentance restores standing. The relationship is healed.
